front.title: Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC

abstract.t: This document specifies multicast delivery mechanisms and catalog-based endpoint discovery for Media over QUIC (MoQ). It defines how MoQ sessions integrate with IP multicast (SSM, ASM), Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT), and TreeDN for scalable live media delivery. The specification includes a multicast catalog extension for endpoint discovery, delivery path selection across TV, mobile, and browser platforms, and a transport priority hierarchy. This document is container-format agnostic and is referenced by both and .

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Live streaming at scale strains unicast CDN architectures, particularly at 4K/8K bitrates. IP multicast replicates streams at the network layer but lacks widespread ISP deployment. Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides modern real-time media transport over QUIC, yet its unicast model shares the same scaling limits.
This document specifies how MoQ integrates with IP multicast:

ol.li: Delivery paths: Platform-specific multicast reception for TV, mobile, and browser clients
TreeDN integration: Hierarchical multicast distribution trees with AMT relay bridging per
Catalog extension: A container-agnostic multicast endpoint discovery mechanism for MoQ catalogs

section.t: This specification is container-format agnostic and works with MMT , CMAF , LOC , or any other MoQ packaging format.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 .

dl.dd: Isolated Web App -- a Chrome packaging format granting DirectSocket API access

section.name: Delivery Paths
section.t: MoQ content reaches receivers via multiple delivery paths depending on platform capabilities:

tr.td: IWA installation (enterprise policy, side-load, or allowlisting)

section.t: Both native SSM and AMT tunneling require UDP socket access.

TV (ATSC 3.0 / ARIB): Broadcast receivers consume ROUTE/ALC or MMTP natively over RF tuner hardware with FEC decoding and S-TSID parsing per . MoQ integration occurs at the gateway level via TreeDN and AMT / DRIAD .
Android: MulticastSocket with MulticastLock for Wi-Fi multicast. For 5G Broadcast, the device modem provides carrier-grade multicast via MBS middleware .
iOS: NWConnectionGroup (iOS 14+) with Apple-granted entitlement.

Browser (IWA): The DirectSocket API provides UDPSocket with joinGroup()/leaveGroup() for SSM and ASM reception. IWAs require enterprise Chrome policy, Google allowlisting, or manual user installation, limiting consumer reach but serving enterprise, kiosk, and power-user deployments.
Browser (standard): MoQ over WebTransport is the production path for standard browsers. WebCodecs provides sub-100ms decode latency; MSE provides fallback. No special permissions required.

section.name: SSM Group Allocation
section.t: For multi-bitrate ABR streams, each quality level maps to a separate SSM group:
ABR quality switches require SSM group changes (IGMP/MLD leave/join).

section.name: TreeDN Integration
section.t: For large-scale MoQ deployments, TreeDN provides:
MoQ relays MAY operate as TreeDN nodes, forwarding packets over both unicast (QUIC) and multicast (SSM/AMT) paths simultaneously.
When operating as a TreeDN node:

ol.li: Relay receives media over SSM from upstream
Relay serves downstream via QUIC (MoQ) and/or SSM (multicast)
FEC repair is preserved or augmented per local conditions

section.name: ISP Router AMT Deployment
section.t: ISP edge routers can serve as AMT relays, replacing x86 CDN servers with inline packet replication on existing routing silicon . AMT relay capability is built into commodity routing chipsets and requires no additional hardware.
This architecture offloads unicast replication to in-network routers performing either:

ul.li: Native SSM replication where PIM-SSM is enabled
AMT unicast replication per where multicast is unavailable

section.t: The MoQ relay at the edge terminates the multicast/AMT path and serves clients over QUIC/WebTransport.

section.name: IWA Home Gateway Architecture
section.t: An IWA node on a wired device (Chromebox, desktop, mini-PC) can serve as a local multicast-to-MoQ gateway. It receives SSM or AMT multicast via DirectSocket over wired Ethernet, then redistributes content over WebTransport to wireless clients on the local network:
section.artwork: Internet (SSM/AMT) | v [ISP Router w/ AMT Relay] | wired Ethernet v [Chromebox / Desktop IWA] <-- DirectSocket multicast rx | WebTransport (local) v [Phone] [Tablet] [Smart TV] [Laptop]
section.t: This provides: (1) wired reception avoids Wi-Fi jitter, (2) a single multicast stream serves the entire household, (3) wireless clients use standard MoQ/WebTransport with no IWA or special permissions, and (4) the gateway can apply local FEC repair.

section.name: Transport Hierarchy
section.t: MoQ clients select the highest-priority available transport:
Native clients (TV, mobile SDK):
Browser clients:
For each transport tier, if FEC repair tracks are available, subscribers SHOULD subscribe to repair tracks at the same priority.

NOTE: For general consumer web applications, MoQ/WebTransport (Priority 3) is effectively the highest-priority available transport. IWA DirectSocket (Priorities 1-2) is limited to enterprise-managed Chrome and users who side-load the IWA .

section.name: Multicast Catalog Extension
section.t: For tracks available via multicast, the MoQ catalog includes a top-level "multicast" field for endpoint discovery. Two formats are defined: a simple flat format for single-endpoint SSM deployments, and an extended format for multi-endpoint scenarios.

section.name: Simple Multicast Format
section.t: The simple format is a flat object at the catalog root level. This is the RECOMMENDED format for deployments where all tracks share a single SSM multicast group:

dl.dd: Multicast group address. SSM range: 232.0.0.0/8 (IPv4), ff3x::/32 (IPv6). ASM range: 224.0.0.0/4 (IPv4), ff0x::/16 (IPv6).
SSM source IP per . Required for SSM. If omitted, implies ASM.

section.t: Subscribers receiving a catalog with the simple multicast format SHOULD auto-connect to the multicast group when multicast APIs are available.

section.name: Extended Multicast Format
section.t: For deployments with multiple multicast groups (per-quality ABR tiers, separate audio/video groups, or multiple TSI values), the extended format uses an "endpoints" array:
Extended endpoint fields:

dl.dd: Transport Session ID for correlation with broadcast signaling (e.g., S-TSID, MMTP).

section.name: Format Detection
section.t: Subscribers MUST detect the multicast format by checking:
Publishers MUST NOT include both "groupAddress" and "endpoints" in the same catalog.

section.name: Network Source Types
section.t: The "networkSource" field describes how subscribers reach the multicast stream when native IP multicast routing is unavailable. It appears at the "multicast" level in both formats.
type (string, REQUIRED): Delivery technology identifier.

section.name: AMT (Automatic Multicast Tunneling)

dl.dd: "driad": DNS Reverse IP AMT Discovery per (RECOMMENDED). "manual": Relay address in the "relay" field.

section.t: Subscribers SHOULD attempt relay discovery in this order:

ol.li: Use DRIAD on the SSM source IP if "discovery" is "driad" or omitted

section.name: ATSC 3.0 (Broadcast Television)
section.t: Receivers with ATSC 3.0 tuner hardware can receive the stream directly over RF per .

section.name: 5G Broadcast (3GPP MBS)
section.t: Android devices with MBS-capable modems receive via MBS middleware .

section.name: Multiple Network Sources
section.t: When a stream is available via multiple delivery technologies, "networkSource" MAY be an array:
Subscribers select the highest-priority available source per .

section.name: Multicast Security
section.t: SSM limits traffic to authorized sources via (S,G) filtering. Additional mechanisms:

ul.li: DTLS-SRTP: Encrypt media payloads when confidentiality is required over multicast.
Source Authentication: Validate source IP at AMT relay; for SSM, receivers verify (S,G) membership.
FEC Integrity: FEC decoder inconsistencies can detect injection attacks; receivers SHOULD verify decoded media structure.
Replay Protection: Sequence numbers enable detection of replayed or reordered packets.
AMT Relay Trust: Trust delegated to relay authentication per .

Content Authentication: Publishers MAY include an Auth Tag MoQ extension header per on source track objects. The auth tag (e.g., ALTA) enables receivers to verify content integrity across untrusted relay chains, complementing SSM source address validation and QUIC hop-by-hop integrity. The authentication scheme and tag size are signaled in the catalog.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions. The multicast catalog extension () is a JSON schema extension to MoQ catalogs and does not require IANA registration.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
DNS Reverse IP Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT) Discovery
TreeDN: Tree-Based Content Delivery Network (CDN) for Live Streaming to Mass Audiences
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
TreeDN - The Fix for Catastrophically Successful Live Streaming Events

section.name: Simple Catalog (Single Multicast Endpoint)
section.t: Catalog with FEC and multicast for a single SSM group:

Subscribers auto-discover the multicast endpoint from the catalog and join the SSM group when multicast APIs are available. The "networkSource" field directs subscribers to the AMT relay when native multicast is unavailable.

section.name: Extended Catalog (Multiple Endpoints)
section.t: Catalog with multiple multicast groups for per-quality ABR tiers:
Subscribers MUST check for simple format fields ("groupAddress", "port") first, then the "endpoints" array.
